cards.headline: Supreme Court blocks Trump from firing Fed governor
cards.summary: The US Supreme Court ruled that Trump's firing of Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook was unconstitutional, in a decision seen as a win for central bank independence. Separately, the court also ruled 6-3 that Trump can fire leaders of other independent agencies, striking down a 91-year-old precedent. The two rulings together signal a mixed but consequential reshaping of presidential power over regulatory bodies.

quiz.prompt: What did the Supreme Court rule regarding Trump's firing of Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook?
options: It was fully within presidential authority
It was referred back to Congress
It was approved on national security grounds
quiz.explanation: The Supreme Court ruled that Trump's firing of Lisa Cook was unconstitutional, sending the fight over her removal back to the lower courts.

cards.headline: Court grants Trump power to fire independent agency chiefs
cards.summary: In a 6-3 decision, the Supreme Court struck down a 91-year-old precedent that had prevented presidents from removing members of independent agencies designed to check executive power. The case centered on the White House's March 2025 firing of Federal Trade Commission member Rebecca Slaughter. Legal and labor experts say the ruling upends settled constitutional law in favor of a presidential loyalty test.

quiz.prompt: Which FTC member's firing was at the center of the Supreme Court's ruling on presidential removal power?
quiz.explanation: The case focused on the White House's March 2025 firing of Federal Trade Commission member Rebecca Slaughter.

cards.headline: US banks clear Fed stress test; buybacks surge
cards.summary: The Federal Reserve's annual stress test found that all 32 large US banks could withstand $708 billion in hypothetical losses. Following the results, JPMorgan Chase unveiled a $50 billion share buyback and Goldman Sachs raised its dividend. Unlike previous years, the results will not directly affect capital requirements, as the Fed is overhauling its rules.

quiz.prompt: How many large banks passed the Federal Reserve's annual stress test?
quiz.explanation: The Fed's annual stress test found that all 32 large banks weathered a hypothetical recession scenario.

cards.headline: Trump threatens 100% tariff over European tech tax
cards.summary: President Trump threatened a 100% tariff on European nations that move forward with a digital services tax on US tech companies. The EU and China, meanwhile, agreed to enter three months of talks aimed at making their bilateral trade relationship more balanced, with the EU running a €360 billion trade deficit with China. The EU also introduced a €3 customs charge on small parcels worth under €150, targeting platforms like Temu and Shein.

quiz.prompt: What tariff level did Trump threaten against European nations over a digital services tax?
quiz.explanation: Trump threatened a 100% tariff on European nations discussing a digital services levy on US tech companies.

cards.headline: Comcast to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky
cards.summary: Comcast announced plans to spin off its media operations, including NBCUniversal and Sky, into a separate business distinct from its mobile and broadband divisions. The move raises questions about the future of Sky News and could set each company up for potential mergers and acquisitions. Analysts note there may not be many good deal options available in the current market.

quiz.prompt: Which media assets is Comcast planning to spin off into a separate company?
quiz.explanation: Comcast announced it would spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a separate media business, splitting from its cable and broadband operations.

cards.headline: British American Tobacco slashes 9,000 jobs for AI
cards.summary: British American Tobacco announced plans to cut approximately 9,000 jobs, representing about a fifth of its total workforce. The company said the cuts are aimed at reducing costs and becoming more technology-enabled and AI-focused. BAT had signaled earlier this year that it was planning savings to make the business more digital.

quiz.prompt: Roughly what share of its workforce is British American Tobacco cutting?
quiz.explanation: British American Tobacco plans to cut about 9,000 jobs, representing approximately one fifth of its workforce.

cards.headline: South Korea unveils $880bn chip and AI plan
cards.summary: South Korea announced an $880 billion investment plan focused on semiconductors and artificial intelligence. The move comes as regional rivals including Taiwan, China, and Japan are all investing heavily in chip factories and related technologies. The scale of the plan underscores the intensifying global competition for dominance in advanced technology.

quiz.prompt: How much is South Korea's new chip and AI investment plan worth?
quiz.explanation: South Korea unveiled an $880 billion investment plan in chips and AI as regional rivals ramp up their own technology spending.

cards.headline: Investors question whether AI is 'one big bubble'
cards.summary: Investors have been selling off AI-related stocks as doubts grow over whether the massive spending on artificial intelligence will generate sufficient returns. A Princeton computer scientist separately argued that AI-driven mass layoffs are being used as a convenient excuse by companies. Ford also reversed course on AI quality checks, rehiring experienced human engineers after finding AI cameras failed to match the skill of veteran technicians.

quiz.prompt: Why did Ford rehire human engineers after deploying AI for quality checks?
options: AI was too expensive to maintain
AI checks failed to match the skill of veteran technicians
quiz.explanation: Ford found that its AI camera systems for design and manufacturing checks were prone to pitfalls and failed to match the quality of experienced human engineers.

cards.headline: Medicare to cover obesity drugs for first time
cards.summary: Medicare will begin covering obesity drugs for the first time, a move that could unlock millions of new patients for Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly. The landmark coverage shift is set to start on July 1, though many seniors may be unaware of the change due to limited advertising from the government or the drug companies. The development significantly expands access to medications that were previously out of reach for most seniors.

quiz.prompt: Which two pharmaceutical companies stand to benefit most from Medicare's new obesity drug coverage?
quiz.explanation: The Medicare coverage expansion could unlock millions of new patients for Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, the leading makers of obesity medications.

cards.headline: Visa and Mastercard consortium launches global stablecoin
cards.summary: A consortium that includes Visa and Mastercard has jointly launched a new global stablecoin. The move signals growing mainstream financial industry engagement with digital currencies and blockchain-based payments. The development comes amid broader momentum in crypto infrastructure, including memory chip shortages driving up costs across consumer electronics tied to AI demand.

quiz.prompt: Which two major payments companies are part of the consortium that launched a new global stablecoin?
quiz.explanation: A consortium including Visa and Mastercard jointly launched a new global stablecoin, marking a significant move by mainstream payment networks into digital currencies.

cards.headline: Ford rehires engineers after AI quality checks fail
cards.summary: Ford found that hundreds of AI cameras used for design and manufacturing quality checks failed to match the skill of veteran technicians, prompting the company to rehire human engineers. The so-called 'greybeards' — experienced workers — were brought back after AI proved unreliable for critical inspection tasks. The case has become a talking point about the real-world limits of replacing human expertise with automation.

quiz.prompt: Why did Ford rehire human engineers after deploying AI?
options: AI cameras were too expensive to maintain
AI quality checks failed to match the skill of veteran technicians
Engineers went on strike over AI use
quiz.explanation: Ford found that AI cameras used for quality checks were prone to pitfalls and could not match the skill of experienced veteran technicians.

cards.headline: GTA 6 goes digital-only, no disc included
cards.summary: Rockstar's highly anticipated GTA 6 will cost £70, and physical copies will not contain a disc — instead including only a code for a digital download. The game is expected to be the biggest release of the year, with one analyst predicting it could make $1 billion within an hour. The move raises broader questions about whether gaming is following music and film into an entirely digital future.

quiz.prompt: What will physical copies of GTA 6 contain instead of a disc?
options: A USB drive with the game
A code for a digital download
quiz.explanation: Rockstar confirmed that physical copies of GTA 6 will include a code for a digital download rather than a disc.

cards.headline: Apple and Xbox hike prices, blaming AI costs
cards.summary: Apple has raised prices on some products by nearly 20%, with the company saying it had 'never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly.' Xbox consoles, Nintendo's Switch 2, and Valve's Steam Deck have also seen significant price rises in recent months. Tech firms are broadly pointing to AI-driven demand for components as a key factor behind the increases.

quiz.prompt: How much have Apple raised some of its prices by?
quiz.explanation: Apple said it had 'never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly,' and raised some prices by nearly 20%.

cards.headline: Trump administration partially lifts Anthropic AI export ban
cards.summary: The U.S. government has partially lifted an export ban on Anthropic's most advanced AI model, asserting a new level of control over which companies can access cutting-edge AI. OpenAI has also agreed to let the administration screen users of its new model. The moves signal a significant expansion of U.S. government influence over who can use frontier AI systems globally.

quiz.prompt: What did OpenAI agree to allow the Trump administration to do?
options: Shut down access to its models entirely
Screen users of its new model
quiz.explanation: OpenAI agreed to let the administration screen users of its new model as part of a broader U.S. government assertion of control over advanced AI access.

cards.headline: Australia's child social media ban barely affecting access
cards.summary: A new study found that six months into Australia's world-first social media ban for children, the policy has barely affected young people's access to platforms including Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube. Australia is now planning to double potential fines for platforms that fail to prevent children from holding accounts. Observers say the government is responding to clear evidence that the ban has failed since taking effect.

quiz.prompt: What does Australia plan to do in response to its social media ban failing?
options: Double potential fines for non-compliant platforms
Block social media apps at the network level
Lower the age threshold of the ban
quiz.explanation: Australia plans to double potential fines for social media platforms that fail to prevent Australian children from holding accounts.

cards.headline: South Korea pledges over $550 billion for memory chips
cards.summary: South Korea's two largest memory chip companies have committed over $550 billion to build more memory fabrication labs as the country positions itself as a global AI technology powerhouse. South Korea has also announced plans to spend $1 trillion overall on memory chip production and humanoid robots, targeting commercial humanoid robots by 2028. Memory chip demand remains robust, with U.S. chipmaker Micron's earnings signalling no end in sight to AI-driven demand.

quiz.prompt: By what year does South Korea aim to have commercial humanoid robots?
quiz.explanation: South Korea's investment plan targets commercial humanoid robots by 2028 as part of its broader AI technology ambitions.

cards.headline: WhatsApp to let users chat using usernames
cards.summary: WhatsApp is rolling out a new feature globally that will allow users to connect and chat by swapping usernames rather than sharing phone numbers. The update is set to be rolled out over the next few months. The change addresses a long-standing privacy concern for users who prefer not to share their personal phone numbers with new contacts.

quiz.prompt: What does WhatsApp's new feature allow users to share instead of phone numbers?
quiz.explanation: WhatsApp's new feature lets people connect and chat by swapping usernames, removing the need to share phone numbers.

cards.headline: IBM claims world-first chip breakthrough below 1 nanometre
cards.summary: IBM says it has created the world's first known chip technology below 1 nanometre in size, describing the design as a 'block of flats' architecture for ultra-tiny chips. The breakthrough is significant for the future of computing, though IBM acknowledges it will be some time before the technology is ready for production. Chipmaker shares have surged broadly in the first half of 2026 as AI demand continues to drive investment.

quiz.prompt: How does IBM describe the design concept behind its new sub-1-nanometre chip?
quiz.explanation: IBM described its new chip architecture as a 'block of flats' design, representing the world's first known chip technology below 1 nanometre.

cards.headline: Investors grow uneasy as AI stock sell-off deepens
cards.summary: Investors have been selling off AI-related stocks amid growing doubts about whether massive spending on AI is delivering sufficient returns, with some asking whether the boom is 'one big bubble.' Despite the market nervousness, earnings from major memory chipmakers like Micron signal demand for AI hardware remains strong with no sign of slowing. Chipmaker shares have nonetheless surged broadly in the first half of 2026, complicating the picture.

quiz.prompt: What do Micron's recent earnings suggest about AI chip demand?
options: There is no end in sight to demand
Demand has plateaued at current levels
Demand is shifting away from memory chips
quiz.explanation: Earnings from major U.S. memory chip maker Micron signalled no end in sight to demand for the microchips at the heart of the AI boom.

cards.headline: Tesla tests Cybercab without steering wheel in Austin
cards.summary: Tesla has started testing its Cybercab robotaxi in Austin without pedals or a steering wheel, marking a potential step toward delivering on Elon Musk's long-standing promise of a commercial robotaxi network. The vehicle is being tested on public roads as the company moves closer to a real-world autonomous ride service. The development comes as rival Waymo and Uber have quietly ended their partnership in Phoenix.

quiz.prompt: What is notable about Tesla's Cybercab currently being tested in Austin?
options: It has no pedals or steering wheel
It uses a fixed route only
quiz.explanation: Tesla is testing its Cybercab in Austin without pedals or a steering wheel, moving toward Elon Musk's vision of a commercial robotaxi network.

cards.headline: Supreme Court blocks Trump's birthright citizenship order
cards.summary: The US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 against President Trump's executive order to end birthright citizenship, affirming that nearly all infants born on US soil automatically become citizens. The decision is widely described as a major blow to Trump and a landmark ruling on the high court's final day of its term. It forms part of a broader day of consequential Supreme Court decisions that went largely against the administration.

quiz.prompt: How did the Supreme Court rule on Trump's birthright citizenship order?
options: Unanimously in favor of the order
quiz.explanation: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 against Trump's executive order, blocking the attempt to end birthright citizenship.

cards.headline: Supreme Court expands presidential power over agencies
cards.summary: In a 6-3 decision, the Supreme Court struck down a 91-year-old precedent that had prevented presidents from removing members of independent agencies, significantly expanding executive power. However, the court separately blocked Trump's attempt to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, a decision seen as a win for central bank independence. The rulings capped a dramatic day that delivered Trump one major win and several setbacks.

quiz.prompt: What did the Supreme Court rule regarding independent agency members?
options: Presidents can now remove members of independent agencies
Presidents cannot remove Federal Reserve governors
quiz.explanation: The court struck down a 91-year-old precedent in a 6-3 decision, giving presidents the power to remove members of independent agencies.

cards.headline: Venezuela earthquake death toll surpasses 1,700
cards.summary: Twin earthquakes struck northern Venezuela, devastating La Guaira state and killing more than 1,700 people, with a strong 4.6-magnitude aftershock later rattling the capital Caracas. Among those caught up in the disaster were over 100 people who had just been deported from the United States and were being held in a hotel when the quakes struck. Angry residents have accused the government of negligence in its disaster response.

quiz.prompt: What happened to recently US-deported Venezuelans during the earthquake?
options: They were held in a hotel that was struck during the quakes
They were safely evacuated before the quakes
They were still on a flight when the quakes hit
They were sheltering in a government building
quiz.explanation: More than 100 people just deported from the US were being held in a hotel in La Guaira when the twin earthquakes struck.

cards.headline: Pakistani airstrikes kill dozens of civilians in Afghanistan
cards.summary: Pakistani airstrikes killed at least 28 to 36 civilians in Afghanistan and wounded 160, according to UN and Afghan officials. Pakistan said it was targeting militant hideouts along the border in response to attacks inside Pakistan, but the Taliban government condemned the strikes as an act of aggression and an atrocity. The incident has sharply escalated tensions between the two countries.

quiz.prompt: Why did Pakistan say it carried out airstrikes in Afghanistan?
options: To target militant hideouts following attacks inside Pakistan
To respond to a Taliban border incursion
quiz.explanation: Pakistan said it targeted militant hideouts along the Pakistan-Afghanistan border in response to militant attacks carried out across Pakistan.

cards.headline: US and Iran resume Qatar talks after recent strikes
cards.summary: The United States and Iran exchanged strikes over the weekend, putting their latest ceasefire in jeopardy. President Trump announced that talks with Iran would resume in Qatar, though Iran said it was sending only a technical delegation to discuss the release of frozen Iranian funds rather than high-level diplomatic negotiations. Qatar's foreign ministry confirmed no direct high-level talks between the US and Iran were scheduled.

quiz.prompt: What did Iran say its delegation in Doha would focus on?
options: The release of frozen Iranian funds
quiz.explanation: Iran said it was sending a technical delegation to Doha specifically to discuss the release of frozen Iranian funds, not broader diplomatic negotiations.

cards.headline: Manhunt after bomb injures Ukrainian tycoon in Monaco
cards.summary: An explosive device packed with bolts and pellets was detonated at the entrance to a building in Monaco, injuring three people including a Ukrainian-born tycoon and his wife and child. Monaco prosecutors said there is no evidence of terrorism and that the suspect, who fled the scene, appears to have acted alone. An international manhunt is under way for the perpetrator.

quiz.prompt: What did Monaco prosecutors say about the Monaco bombing suspect?
options: The suspect acted alone and remains at large
The suspect was arrested at the scene
It was a coordinated terrorist attack
The suspect was a known criminal associate
quiz.explanation: Monaco prosecutors said the suspect appeared to have acted alone and had fled the scene, with a manhunt under way.

cards.headline: Anti-CCP Chinese tycoon Guo Wengui jailed 30 years
cards.summary: Exiled Chinese billionaire Guo Wengui, who branded himself a critic of the Chinese Communist Party, has been sentenced to 30 years in a US federal prison after being convicted on nine charges including money laundering and fraud. The judge said Guo diverted investor money to fund a lavish lifestyle, rather than to fight the Communist Party as he claimed. Guo had gained a large following among people who backed his schemes.

quiz.prompt: What did the judge say Guo Wengui did with investor money?
options: Diverted it to fund a lavish lifestyle
Used it to fund anti-CCP activism
quiz.explanation: The judge said Guo diverted investor money to live lavishly, rather than using it to oppose the Chinese Communist Party as he had claimed.

cards.headline: Germany counts nearly 60,000 far-right extremists
cards.summary: Germany's domestic intelligence agency has identified almost 60,000 far-right extremists in the country, with more than a quarter of them believed to be potentially violent. The figure underscores the scale of the challenge facing German authorities in monitoring and containing the far-right movement. The disclosure comes amid ongoing debates in Europe about the rise of extremist movements.

quiz.prompt: What proportion of Germany's identified far-right extremists are believed to be violent?
quiz.explanation: Germany's domestic intelligence agency said more than a quarter of the nearly 60,000 identified far-right extremists are believed to be violent.

cards.headline: South Africa deploys police amid anti-migrant protests
cards.summary: South African authorities deployed police units across towns and cities as anti-migrant groups held rallies demanding undocumented foreigners leave the country. The government fears a repeat of anti-migrant violence seen in 2008, which led to looting and 62 deaths. Foreigners have already begun leaving South Africa ahead of a deadline set by the anti-migrant groups.

quiz.prompt: What event are South African authorities trying to prevent a repeat of?
quiz.explanation: The South African government fears a repeat of anti-migrant violence in 2008 that led to looting and resulted in the deaths of 62 people.

cards.headline: DR Congo bans gatherings as Ebola whereabouts unknown
cards.summary: The Democratic Republic of Congo has banned mass gatherings in its capital to prevent the spread of Ebola, with the whereabouts of nearly 300 people who tested positive for the virus currently unknown. Modelling predicts thousands of deaths in the DRC by September if the outbreak is not contained, with fears of huge community transmission. Opposition politicians have accused the government of using the Ebola outbreak as a pretext to block a planned protest.

quiz.prompt: How many Ebola-positive people are currently unaccounted for in DR Congo?
quiz.explanation: The whereabouts of almost 300 people who tested positive for Ebola in the DRC are currently unknown, raising fears of widespread community transmission.

cards.headline: Germany and Netherlands both exit World Cup on penalties
cards.summary: Four-time winners Germany were knocked out of the 2026 World Cup, losing to Paraguay 4-3 on penalties after a 1-1 draw, ending a remarkable 50-year penalty shootout winning streak in major tournaments. The Netherlands also fell at the same stage, missing three spot kicks as Morocco came from behind to beat them on penalties in Monterrey. Both eliminations on the same day mark a stunning night of European exits from the tournament.

quiz.prompt: How did Paraguay beat Germany in the 2026 World Cup?
options: 5-4 on penalties after a goalless draw
quiz.explanation: Germany and Paraguay drew 1-1 after extra time, with Paraguay winning 4-3 on penalties.

cards.headline: LeBron James leaves Lakers, Warriors move looms
cards.summary: LeBron James has informed the Los Angeles Lakers he will be moving on, opening his free agency after eight seasons with the team. Reports have strongly linked the 41-year-old with a move to the Golden State Warriors, where a potential union with Stephen Curry and Anthony Davis is being discussed. Draymond Green declining his $27.6 million player option has been seen as a signal the Warriors are restructuring to make room.

quiz.prompt: Which Warriors player declined his player option, signaling the team's pursuit of LeBron?
quiz.explanation: Draymond Green declined his $27.6 million player option, which was seen as a move to free up space for a potential LeBron James signing.

cards.headline: Swiatek in tears after nervy Wimbledon title defence begins
cards.summary: Defending champion Iga Swiatek was visibly emotional on court after surviving a stuttering three-set first-round scare against Taylor Townsend to begin her Wimbledon title defence. Swiatek described it as a tough couple of weeks heading into the tournament. Serena Williams also returned to Wimbledon's Centre Court, drawing global attention to the grass-court major's opening days.

quiz.prompt: Who did Iga Swiatek defeat in her opening Wimbledon 2026 match?
quiz.explanation: Swiatek beat Taylor Townsend in a three-set first-round match, crying on court after the victory.

cards.headline: Serena Williams makes emotional return to Wimbledon
cards.summary: Serena Williams returned to Wimbledon's Centre Court, a moment that drew widespread attention given her legacy at the tournament, which includes seven singles titles. Williams had previously come back from retirement before this appearance. Her return was one of the most talked-about moments of the tournament's opening days.

quiz.prompt: How many Wimbledon singles titles has Serena Williams won?
quiz.explanation: According to the snippets, Serena Williams has won seven Wimbledon singles titles as part of her legacy at the tournament.

cards.headline: Martinelli's 95th-minute winner sends Brazil through
cards.summary: Gabriel Martinelli scored a dramatic stoppage-time winner in the 95th minute as Brazil came from behind to beat Japan and advance to the World Cup last 16. The victory sets up a last-16 tie for Brazil against the winner of Norway or Ivory Coast. The late comeback added to an already dramatic day of World Cup knockout action.

quiz.prompt: Who scored Brazil's winning goal against Japan in the 2026 World Cup?
quiz.explanation: Gabriel Martinelli scored in the 95th minute to send Brazil through to the last 16 of the 2026 World Cup.

cards.headline: Ja Morant's fall from NBA stardom is complete
cards.summary: Once touted as the future face of the NBA, Ja Morant has been moved on from Memphis following a combination of off-court problems and injuries. The guard's spectacular rise has been followed by an equally dramatic fall, ending his time with the franchise that made him a star. His exit marks one of the more striking collapses of a young superstar's trajectory in recent NBA history.

quiz.prompt: What factors contributed to Ja Morant's fall from NBA stardom according to reports?
quiz.explanation: The Guardian reports that off-court problems and injuries combined to end Morant's time with Memphis.

cards.headline: Klopp plays down links to Germany head coach role
cards.summary: Following Germany's shock World Cup elimination by Paraguay on penalties, Jürgen Klopp has publicly responded to widespread speculation linking him to the vacant Germany head coach position. Manager Julian Nagelsmann said he would not quit despite the early exit. Germany's elimination has reignited the long-running debate about whether Klopp should take charge of the national team.

quiz.prompt: What was Germany manager Julian Nagelsmann's response to calls for him to resign after the World Cup exit?
options: He said he would not quit
He asked for a review period
quiz.explanation: Despite Germany's elimination, Nagelsmann stated he would not quit as manager.

cards.headline: Supreme Court rules states can ban trans athletes from female sports
cards.summary: The US Supreme Court has ruled that states can exclude transgender athletes from female sports competitions, overturning judgments issued by lower courts. The decision came in cases involving trans students who had sued after being barred from competing in West Virginia and Idaho. The ruling has major implications for how states regulate athletic participation at school and college levels.

quiz.prompt: Which two states were involved in the Supreme Court cases on transgender athletes in female sports?
quiz.explanation: The Supreme Court overturned lower court rulings in cases involving trans students who sued after being barred from competing in West Virginia and Idaho.

cards.headline: Morocco's win over Netherlands sparks Dutch street scenes
cards.summary: Morocco's dramatic penalty shootout victory over the Netherlands in the World Cup last 32 led to celebrations and disorder on Dutch streets, with police pelted with bottles in The Hague. In Amsterdam, there were also hugs between sets of fans after the tense encounter. The Netherlands missed three penalties in the shootout as Morocco advanced to the last 16.

quiz.prompt: How many penalties did the Netherlands miss in their shootout loss to Morocco?
quiz.explanation: The Netherlands missed three spot kicks as Morocco beat them on penalties to reach the World Cup last 16.

cards.headline: Australia thrash West Indies to reach T20 World Cup final
cards.summary: Australia advanced to the ICC Women's T20 World Cup final with a convincing eight-wicket win over the West Indies at The Oval. They will face either England or South Africa in the final. The emphatic victory underlined Australia's dominance in the women's T20 format.

quiz.prompt: By what margin did Australia beat the West Indies in the Women's T20 World Cup semi-final?
quiz.explanation: Australia beat the West Indies by eight wickets to reach the Women's T20 World Cup final.

cards.headline: Supreme Court blocks Trump's birthright citizenship order
cards.summary: The US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to uphold birthright citizenship, blocking President Trump's executive order that sought to end the automatic citizenship granted to nearly all infants born on US soil. The decision was described as a major blow to Trump's agenda and came on the final day of the court's term. Civil rights groups and Democrats welcomed the ruling.

quiz.prompt: How did the Supreme Court rule on Trump's birthright citizenship executive order?
options: Sent it back to lower courts
quiz.explanation: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to block Trump's executive order, affirming that nearly all infants born on US soil automatically become citizens.

cards.headline: Supreme Court strikes down political party spending limits
cards.summary: The Supreme Court struck down a post-Watergate law that had limited the amount individuals can give to political parties for coordinated spending. The case stemmed from a 2022 lawsuit challenging Federal Election Commission enforcement of those limits. Critics argue the ruling further undermines Congress's ability to regulate money in politics.

quiz.prompt: What law did the Supreme Court strike down in its campaign finance ruling?
options: A post-Watergate law limiting coordinated party spending
quiz.explanation: The Court struck down a post-Watergate law that Congress had passed to limit the amount individuals can give to political parties for coordinated expenditures.

cards.headline: Supreme Court upholds state bans on transgender athletes
cards.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that states can ban transgender athletes from competing in women's and girls' sports, overturning lower court judgments that had favored trans students who sued after being barred in West Virginia and Idaho. Justice Brett Kavanaugh wrote the majority opinion. The ruling was one of several major decisions handed down on the final day of the court's term.

quiz.prompt: Which justice wrote the Supreme Court's majority opinion on transgender athletes in sports?
quiz.explanation: Justice Brett Kavanaugh wrote the majority opinion upholding state bans on transgender athletes competing in women's and girls' sports.

cards.headline: Manhunt underway after bomb injures Ukrainian oligarch in Monaco
cards.summary: An explosive device containing bolts and pellets detonated in Monaco, injuring a Ukrainian oligarch. The head of Monaco's government confirmed the nature of the device, and prosecutors said there is no evidence of terrorism. A suspect fled the scene and a manhunt is underway.

quiz.prompt: What did Monaco prosecutors say about the nature of the blast that injured a Ukrainian oligarch?
options: There was no terrorism evidence found
quiz.explanation: Monaco prosecutors said there was no evidence of terrorism after the explosion, which was caused by a device containing bolts and pellets.

cards.headline: US envoys in Doha for indirect Iran nuclear talks
cards.summary: US envoys traveled to Doha for indirect talks, with Iran sending a technical delegation to follow up on a memorandum of understanding deal and the release of frozen Iranian funds. Qatar's foreign ministry confirmed no high-level or direct US-Iran meetings are scheduled. The talks mark a continued diplomatic channel between the two countries.

quiz.prompt: What did Qatar's foreign ministry say about direct US-Iran meetings in Doha?
options: They were confirmed at the highest level
quiz.explanation: Qatar's foreign ministry spokesman confirmed that no high-level meetings or direct talks between the US and Iran were scheduled.

cards.headline: US murder rate approaches record all-time low
cards.summary: The US murder rate is approaching a record low, reversing fears from a few years ago that elevated violent crime had become a 'new normal.' Experts had previously worried after a spike in homicides during the pandemic era. The trend marks a significant shift in the country's public safety landscape.

quiz.prompt: What are experts saying about the current US murder rate?
options: It is at a historic high
It is approaching a record low
It has remained flat for a decade
It has risen sharply this year
quiz.explanation: The US murder rate is approaching a record low, reversing earlier fears of a 'new normal' of elevated violent crime.

cards.headline: UK announces £300 billion defence investment plan
cards.summary: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a £300 billion defence investment plan, which includes more than £5 billion for drones and autonomous systems over four years. The plan is designed to help the UK hit NATO's spending targets. BBC Verify has been examining what the large numbers mean for military spending pledges.

quiz.prompt: How much of the UK's new defence plan is earmarked specifically for drones and autonomous systems?
quiz.explanation: The UK defence investment plan includes more than £5 billion for drones and autonomous systems over four years.

cards.headline: Venezuelans accuse government of neglect after earthquakes
cards.summary: People in areas devastated by twin earthquakes in Venezuela say rescuers have not been sent and they need more support from the government. The disaster is being seen as a test of state capacity in a country already struggling with years of crisis. Advocacy groups have also raised concerns about deportees sent to Ghana amid separate legal disputes.

quiz.prompt: What are Venezuelans in earthquake-affected areas accusing their government of?
quiz.explanation: People in devastated areas said no rescuers had been sent and accused the Venezuelan government of negligence in responding to the twin earthquakes.

cards.headline: UK inquiry finds racism harming maternity patient safety
cards.summary: An independent inquiry found 'unacceptable racism and discrimination' affecting patient safety in UK maternity care, with the government promising to act on failings described as shaming society. The findings highlight systemic issues in how maternity services are delivered across the country. A Sky News interactive tool has also mapped the state of maternity care by postcode.

quiz.prompt: What did the independent maternity care inquiry in the UK find?
options: Underfunding was the sole cause of failings
Unacceptable racism and discrimination affecting patient safety
quiz.explanation: The independent inquiry found 'unacceptable racism and discrimination' was affecting patient safety in UK maternity care.

cards.headline: World Cup matches played in potentially dangerous heat
cards.summary: Nine group-stage matches at the 2026 World Cup were played amid potentially dangerous heat conditions, according to analysis cited by the players' union. The union has pointed to the findings as a warning for the broader sport and called for lessons to be learned. The issue has reignited debate about player welfare in high-temperature playing conditions.

quiz.prompt: How many World Cup group-stage games were played in potentially dangerous heat?
quiz.explanation: Analysis found that nine group-stage matches at the 2026 World Cup were played amid potentially dangerous heat conditions.
